posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
My young associate said, “it seems to have a verdant streak to it”.

Now mind you, this is not a wine guy. He just happens to be one of the most brilliant associates that I have hired, a math geek musician, brainiac, and totally cool as well. We both encouraged our wives to go out of town for vacay - an easy tennis resort break for my lovely wife - as we are cranking for a major trial. Had him over for dinner, grilled filet, roasted root vegetables, steamed asparagus, etc. Started with a 2010 Copain Halcon Syrah and then segued to a lovely 1995 Sociando Mallet that had been decanted for about two hours.

The wine exhibited that archetype Sociando nose of a mild herbaceous streak, ash, tobacco and cassis. Medium bodied, completely at apogee, with a forest floor base of earth and dried ground cover, menthol, and range of crispy red fruits to some plummy darks. Modest finish with tannins resolved. A really nice, mature Bordeaux, just doesn’t take it to the next level. The mid-palate is a little watery and diffuse and the finish is short. A solid, worker’s Bordeaux, but this particular bottle was not at the same level of some recent vintages I have popped, like 1989, 1990, 1996 and 2000, over the last 6 months. Happy to have more of this, however, given the value.

(91 pts.)

posts.name: Marc Frontario
posts.cooked: Sorry I couldn’t make over Robert…I’m quite sure u would have been impressed by my extensive, diverse, expressive descriptors like, green and leafy notes.

posts.name: Warren Taranow
posts.cooked: There was a recent post with lots of love for the ‘95 Left Bankers in general. Most of mine have had a low ratio of fruit (and flavor) to tannins. I drank a ‘95 SM a couple of years ago, and thought the last glass was best, but like yours, it never really reached the tier of other vintages. In a slight drift (of vintage), I had an awful run of bad ‘85s and ‘86s.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Warren I’m with you on the 95s. I guess time will tell but the vintage has not wowed me like it did the critics and some current commentary.
Sociando = the perfect wine for asparagus. Unless that is too matchy-matchy

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: It’s like a plaid tie with a plaid jacket! So hip. You gotta look good to pull it off, though.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: A bottle I had in France last year was a bit more dramatic than this note makes the wine sound. I’ll have to open another one soon to see if it was a one off.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
You know what they say about great bottles…

I have a few more coming in from a different source, so will pop one in the near-term.

I will admit to having struggles with this vintage, perhaps 22 years is just not enough. I don’t know.

Robert Alfert:

we are cranking for a major trial. Had him over for dinner, grilled filet, roasted root vegetables, steamed asparagus, etc. Started with a 2010 Copain Halcon Syrah and then segued to a lovely 1995 Sociando Mallet that had been decanted for about two hours.

This is trial prep I could adjust to!

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

You know what they say about great bottles…

I have a few more coming in from a different source, so will pop one in the near-term.

I will admit to having struggles with this vintage, perhaps 22 years is just not enough. I don’t know.

Not being facetious, but i think your last comment is probably correct. Ive been doing this since the75-76 vintages and have decided that in significant years many of the wines just aren’t really there yet until 30 years of age.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
john stimson:
Robert Alfert:

You know what they say about great bottles…

I have a few more coming in from a different source, so will pop one in the near-term.

I will admit to having struggles with this vintage, perhaps 22 years is just not enough. I don’t know.

Not being facetious, but i think your last comment is probably correct. Ive been doing this since the75-76 vintages and have decided that in significant years many of the wines just aren’t really there yet until 30 years of age.

True, and intuitively and empirically, I know that, but still am confounded from time to time from some vintages. Vintages 1975, 1988, 1995, and maybe 1986, fall in line there. And then some others, like 1982 and 1990 have always seemed to show well, and even 2000, which still needs more time, will show better, sooner, than these tougher vintages.

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked: Had this last night. Really friggin good. Your note nailed it, except I think I liked it a bit more (perhaps due to lower expectations). I prefer it to the 2000 S-M, at least for current drinking. In a great place, but I’m guessing it holds at this level for a long while.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked: I’ve 3 of these stashed in the offsite. Looks like I’ll keep it there for bit more.
